summary: This notebook demonstrates a synthetic data generation and inference pipeline for a mathematics misconception detection competition. It loads test questions and answer options, formats them into structured prompts with chain-of-thought instructions, and generates responses using a quantized LLM via vLLM. The generated text is then embedded with a pre-trained BGE model and matched against a predefined misconception mapping using cosine similarity to produce final predictions. The approach highlights how to efficiently run large language models on limited GPU resources and leverage semantic embeddings for zero-shot label mapping. Readers should take away practical techniques for prompt engineering, AWQ quantization, and embedding-based classification without fine-tuning.
competition_flow: Loads test CSV data, formats it into prompts for a quantized LLM, generates misconception responses via vLLM, embeds the text with BGE, matches against a misconception mapping via cosine similarity, and outputs a formatted submission CSV.
data_reading: pd.read_csv for test data and misconception mapping
pd.read_parquet for intermediate and output files
data_processing: Melts wide answer columns to long format
insights: AWQ quantization combined with vLLM enables efficient LLM inference on limited GPU memory.
Explicit chain-of-thought prompting with structured XML-like tags improves reliable output extraction.
Pre-trained BGE embeddings can effectively map generated text to predefined categories via cosine similarity without task-specific fine-tuning.
